Chapter 1: TheLibrary Chapter Notes 11/17/2019: 2nd beta pass by guestuser28653. And yes, I really do love that screen name. :D In the summer before his fifth year at Hogwarts, Harry found himself staring at a door that Hermione had said wouldnŐt open for anyone. After the Dementor fiasco, Harry arrived at Grimmauld Place, and Hermione and Ron filled Harry in on their summer. Harry hadnŐt been happy with either of them; heŐd much rather have been cleaning doxies and dark artifacts with his friends than weeding the DursleysŐ garden or washing the DursleysŐ floors for the third time in one week. Even more irritating was the fact that their lack of communication with him had apparently been by DumbledoreŐs orders. Dumbledore, who hadnŐt even bothered to look him in the eye during his trial, had effectively commanded his friends to abandon him. Even more hurtful, theyŐd listened. HeŐd made a half-assed attempt at hiding his irritation before his trial. After the trial, however, DumbledoreŐs completely apathetic attitude towards Harry made him not bother holding it in any longer. And on top of everything else, Ron and Hermione had been chosen for prefects of Gryffindor, while Harry, who had actually come face-to-face with Voldemort only months earlier, had nothing to show for it except a series of articles in the Prophet that portrayed him as ÔThe Boy Who Is Touched In the Head.Ő All of this had led him to be rather sour with Ron and Hermione. He knew he took his anger out on the wrong people, but it made Harry feel better. After that, it seemed, theyŐd taken to avoiding him. Harry was oddly okay with this. The more they avoided him the less his scar prickled. So heŐd formed a new hobby of wandering around Grimmauld Place, staring at the oddities and peering at old portraits until he came across the door that Hermione mustŐve been talking about. He knew she said it was on the top floor, at the end of a dimly lit hall. There was something strange about the door. Harry couldnŐt quite put his finger on what it was, but he found himself feeling drawn to it. To be honest, he wasnŐt even sure why he went to the top floor in the first place; his feet just seemed to have taken him up the stairs. He knew that should probably have alarmed him considering he was in a house chock full of dark artifacts, but the door didnŐt feel threatening. He reached out and tried the knob, and the door opened. Harry blinked, then wondered if it was something like a pickle jar in a Muggle sitcom; everyone trying the door earlier in the summer had loosened something so it could open for Harry. As he poked his head in the door to peer in, a few torches around the perimeter of the room flared to life, and he saw walls lined floor to ceiling with books. A few chairs and a desk resided in the middle. HarryŐs first instinct was to get Hermione. After all, it was clearly a library, probably full of books Hermione had never heard of. But then he remembered that he was still irritated with his friends, and he stepped inside to explore without them. He could tell them later. The torches seemed to brighten as he neared one of the shelves. He ran his finger along the spines of a few books; they were clearly old, but they seemed to be in good condition, although many didnŐt have a title or author printed on the spine. He pulled one off of the shelf at random and flipped it open, and the text almost seemed to leap out at him. Shadow Walking is a branch of shadow magic that can be immensely useful in a variety of applications. It is one of the first magics a shadow mage will learn and is a staple of the mageŐs arsenal, as one must master shadow walking prior to commanding shadows to shift. Obviously, to begin learning shadow walking, and therefore to begin learning shadow magic, one must have already completed the declaration rites of being a dark witch or wizard. It is recommended that the next step .Harry stopped reading, startled. He flipped the book to the front cover, and a title appeared. Introduction to Specialized Arts of the Dark Path, it read. He turned the book over to glance at the spine of the book and then looked back up at the shelves, realizing that he could now read all of the titles, as if it just took time for his eyes to adjust. As he took in the titles -Infusing Flame and Darkness, Mind Arts and Dark Magic, and Nearly Undetectable Curses and Hexes, to name a few - he abruptly realized that the entire library was dedicated to the dark arts. He knew that should terrify him, but it didnŐt. Harry chalked up his lack of alarm to knowing what the house was. Sirius had told him that his family had been Voldemort sympathizers, at the least, and Hermione and Ron had said that theyŐd been disposing of dark artifacts all summer. He knew he should go get someone and let them know what heŐd found, but something made him hesitate. There might be something useful buried in here, Harry thought. After what happened in the graveyard at the end of last year, Harry didnŐt want to risk losing something he could potentially use against Voldemort. In the back of his mind, Harry knew that was a strange thought for him to have, but chalked it up to wanting to use anything and everything at his disposal after seeing Voldemort resurrected. He supposed that DumbledoreŐs complete lack of information likely wasnŐt helping, either. Harry felt like if he found himself alone as he had in the graveyard, he wanted to be able to do more than a mere Ôexpelliarmus.Ő Voldemort killed HarryŐs parents, after all. Cedric died because of him. Harry didnŐt want to lose anyone else, and if there was something that could help him save even one person... Harry pulled Undetectable Curses from the shelves and the book he already had in his hands fell to the ground and opened to a different chapter. He was just reaching down to retrieve it when something in the text caught his eye. Dark healers tend to be much more proficient in healing damage caused by dark curses; in fact, only dark healers have managed to fully cure curse scars, which are scars left by extremely dark magic, whether on purpose or as unintended side effects. If they are unable to cure a curse scar, dark healers are far more willing than a standard healer or medi-wizard to remove the affected part of the victimŐs body .Harry found himself slowly sinking to the floor. He set the book on curses aside and kept reading, wondering if the book would cover exactly what curse scars were. HeŐd been told his own scar was a curse scar more times than he could count, but no one had ever bothered to explain exactly what that meant. *** A few hours passed before Sirius found him. Harry sat cross-legged on the floor and had completely surrounded himself with books by that point, having used bibliographies to track down other books mentioned in the ones heŐd already skimmed. HeŐd gone from reading about curse scars to dark protections to wards that could be set to activate depending on the attacker's intent, and gone right on to flip through various volumes, trying to find more references to something called 'soul magicks.' Above all else, it seemed like the dark arts were just a different branch of magic, and just like the magic heŐd learned at Hogwarts, it could be used for right or wrong purposes. Sure, there were some fairly awful rituals mentioned, but many - like some of the dark healing spells heŐd found in Specialized Arts of the Dark Path - seemed even more helpful than some of the spells heŐd learned at school. Harry knew that he was likely reading biased material but he kept wondering how dark arts could really be so evil and bad like heŐd been told since heŐd been introduced to the wizarding world. WasnŐt something like Ôdark healingŐ an oxymoron? ŇHarry?Ó Harry looked up at Sirius, startled to see his godfather standing in the doorway. ŇSirius! I was just -Ó ŇThe door opened for you?Ó There was an expression on SiriusŐs face that Harry couldnŐt interpret. Harry shrugged. ŇYeah. I mean, everyone else trying earlier probably justÉ loosened something.Ó Sirius still had that strange look on his face. ŇRight.Ó Harry felt like he was being judged, and he instantly became defensive. ŇI was justÉ I meanÉ I found something about curse scars, and nobody ever explained to me about mine, and IÉÓ He trailed off, feeling awkward, and mentally prepared himself for a lecture from Sirius. Sirius just stared at him for a few long moments, and Harry felt even more awkward. Harry grew uncomfortable, and he had to drop his eyes to the floor. It made sense that Sirius would be disappointed to find Harry surrounded by books on dark arts; after all, Sirius left his family because of their immersion in the dark arts... hadnŐt he? Sirius finally broke the silence. ŇIs that ÔNearly Undetectable Curses and HexesŐ over there?Ó He let out a laugh, and Harry looked up again. ŇI absolutely loved that book when I was in Hogwarts! Reg had to get me my own copy because I kept stealing his.Ó Sirius finally entered the room and sat down on the floor in front of Harry, picking up the book in question. He leaned over into a half-lying position, propping himself up on one elbow, and he flipped through the book while Harry watched, dumbfounded. Sirius came to a certain page heŐd apparently been looking for and let out another laugh, more jovial than the last. ŇThis one .carinitus - I used that one on your father more than a few times in the Gryffindor common room. ItŐs basically a pantsing hex. He never caught me at it, but I had a feeling he knew it was me.Ó Sirius grinned at Harry. ŇI can teach it to you, if youŐd like, just as long as you promise to never cast it on a girl.Ó Harry felt stunned. ŇSirius, isnŐt itÉÓ ŇDark arts?Ó Sirius thankfully finished HarryŐs question for him. ŇTechnically. Barely.Ó He rolled his eyes. ŇYes.Ó He sighed. ŇI mean, itŐs one of the least harmful spells in this book.Ó He flipped through another few pages and pointed out a different spell. ŇThis is actually one James asked me to teach him. Flips people up in the air and upside down. He liked using it on Snivellus, until it came out that Death Eaters had been using it on Muggles -Ó ŇWait,Ó Harry said. ŇAre you saying my dad used dark arts?Ó Sirius shook his head. ŇJames didnŐt know it was dark until I told him. I know I probably should have told him, but I didnŐt know how anti-dark arts he was until after I taught him, and by then heŐd been using the hex for over a yearÉÓ He sighed. ŇThat was the second biggest argument we ever had.Ó For the first time since stepping into the library, Harry felt guilty. He knew his father likely hadnŐt been a fan of the dark arts, but if he had gotten mad at his best friend over something so small, he probably wouldnŐt be a fan of Harry surrounding himself with books on the dark arts. Harry was also confused. ŇButÉ didnŐt you leave your family because of their practicing dark arts?Ó he asked. ŇThe way you were talking about this houseÉ and themÉÓ ŇNo,Ó Sirius replied. ŇI left because they supported Voldemort.Ó He sighed again. ŇThereŐs also dark arts, and then there are black arts that can eat away at your soul or start affecting your mind. My mother was really into that kind of magic, and she kept trying to drag Reg into itÉÓ ŇSoÉ someone that practices dark arts isnŐt necessarily a dark wizard?Ó Harry asked. ŇUh, thatŐsÉÓ Sirius coughed. ŇWell, dark witches and wizards arenŐt necessarily evil, and Ôdark wizardŐ also doesnŐt mean a follower of the so-called ÔDark Lord.Ő Dark just means dark, Harry.Ó He glanced up at the set of shelves behind Harry. ŇActually, thereŐs a book hereÉÓ He stood up and started scouring the titles, obviously looking for something specific. ŇI was probably around eight years old when I read it, but seeing as how youŐre just now looking into thisÉ ah-ha!Ó He pulled a thinner book from the shelves and passed it to Harry. ŇIf youŐre really interested in all this, you should probably start here. I am many things, but a professor is not one of them.Ó Harry slid his thumb under the title: An Introduction to the Dark Arts: The Power, Prejudice, and Politics. Harry grinned and he immediately flipped the book open to skim the table of contents. He felt like he could suddenly understand HermioneŐs excitement over books. ŇHarry.Ó He looked up to see Sirius still watching him. ŇDo me a favor. Please donŐt tell anyone about this room. I may hate this house - and I mean, I really hate this house - but thereŐs a lot of knowledge here that canŐt be found anywhere else. The Black Library is one of the best family libraries in all of Britain.Ó He sighed. ŇBut some people wonŐt care about that, and theyŐd just want to destroy everything in here.Ó Harry quickly agreed; after all, in addition to the possibility that he could find something useful in the library, he found that he quite enjoyed the few hours he spent there. If he had to admit it, he felt more at peace in that room than heŐd felt all summer. ŇYou donŐt have to read that now, though,Ó Sirius said, grinning. ŇThereŐs something else cool about this library. If youŐre okay with learning them, how about I teach you a few of those undetectable hexes?Ó *** As it turned out, the library had what Sirius called an Ôisolation ward,Ő which Harry thought sounded like something from a hospital, but actually meant that Harry was able to use magic in it without setting off the Ministry trace. Due to what had happened with the Dementors only a week prior, Harry asked Sirius at least ten times if he was sure that the ward still worked, and Sirius told him to cast a simple ÔlumosŐ and to wait. He cast one, they waited, and no owls appeared. Sirius may not have been a professor, but he was brilliant at practical teaching. He showed Harry a few of the more fun and creative spells from Nearly Undetectable Hexes and Curses, and to the astonishment of Sirius, Harry could cast them successfully after only a few attempts. He was already planning on trying them out on Malfoy once school started. They practiced together until dinner, and he was unable to stop grinning. Mrs. Weasley was the only one who dared to comment on HarryŐs improvement in his mood. ŇYouŐve been in a right state since you got here, Harry,Ó she said. ŇItŐs nice to see a smile on your face.Ó Harry returned to the library the following day, and he started and finished the dark arts introduction book. Mrs. Weasley scolded him for disappearing when it was time to help with cleaning Grimmauld Place, but he didnŐt care. The book was fascinating and enlightening. From what Harry gathered from the book, until recently, dark wizards and witches had been considered more like a Muggle religion, or maybe even similar to a Muggle political party, than how they were currently viewed - dark cloaks skulking in the shadows, laughing maniacally, and plotting world domination. The Dark Lord GrindelwaldŐs actions had sullied the reputation of dark witches and wizards across all of Europe and Asia. Although the book had clearly been written before VoldemortŐs rise, he supposed that Voldemort hadnŐt helped their reputation, either. Harry also learned that using ÔdarkŐ magic was simply pulling on a different type of magic than the spells that he learned in school. Dark magic and light magic were the two biggest magic Ôfamilies,Ő but there were other types that werenŐt studied as much, such as earth magic or wild magic. Something else Harry found interesting was that it turned out to be true that the majority of dark wizards resided in Slytherin House at Hogwarts. Although it wasnŐt all of them - there were a fair number in Ravenclaw, as well - the sullied reputation of dark witches and wizards was enough to have earned Slytherin the identical reputation as the ÔevilŐ and ÔbadŐ house. As Hogwarts began banning the use of all dark arts - including the more helpful arts - the split became even more prominent. And as for dark wizards themselves, Harry discovered that a wizard wasnŐt truly considered ÔdarkŐ simply because they practiced dark arts. They actually performed ceremonies before they could declare themselves dark, like some kind of rite of passage. After a dark wizard officially declared, they would have easier access to the dark arts and they could potentially become more powerful, depending on the witch or wizard. However, sometimes wizards could become temperamental, as dark magic was rather volatile. It was apparently important that a dark wizard work with the dark magic, rather than try to control it - or worse - allowing the magic to control them. This didnŐt dissuade Harry, and he also began trying more of the spells from the Undetectable Curses book. He learned a curse that made a victim unable to sleep, as well as a curse that would result in the victim becoming a magnet for a creature of the casterŐs choice, such as spiders. Ron would keel over, Harry thought absently. He wasn't actually able to test the success of those curses, so he tried some of the hexes. He perfected something called an Ôelemental mineŐ in one afternoon - laying down an invisible glyph on the ground, and when someone walked over it, it could freeze or electrocute or burn the victim, depending on what spellwork was worked in with the glyph. At some point, Harry realized that he was throwing himself headfirst into learning about the dark arts. He chalked it up to just wanting to learn any and every way he could defend himself against Voldemort, but there was something about it all that just felt right to him. He wanted to learn everything he can. Harry was scouring the shelves to see if he could find any dark defensive spells when Sirius came into the library. ŇHarry,Ó Sirius said, both his tone and face rather grim. ŇMcGonagall is here to see you.Ó ŇWhat?Ó Harry asked. ŇWhy?Ó *** ŇSimply put, Potter, youŐve been expelled,Ó McGonagall said. A bolt of pain flashed through his scar. ŇWhat the fuck -Ó Harry started to say, but McGonagall cut him off. ŇAs we are not currently in school I will allow that comment to slide, Potter, but really -Ó ŇWell, since IŐve been bloody expelled itŐs not like you can take points, is it?Ó Harry said rather nastily. Sirius let out a bark of a laugh from the corner of the dining room. ŇMr. Potter!Ó McGonagall snapped. ŇIf you would allow me to finish what IŐm saying, you could fully understand the situation youŐre in. Then you may make your smart comments. Please have a seat.Ó Harry supposed that was McGonagallŐs way of saying Ôsit down and shut up.Ő He sat at the table, and McGonagall pulled out the chair across from him. ŇIn short, you were expelled after receiving the underage magic notice from the Ministry of Magic,Ó she said. ŇIt was an error. It should never have happened, and the decision has obviously been reversed.Ó ŇThenÉ why are you here?Ó Harry asked hesitantly. ŇBecause when the magic of the school detected that you had been expelled, you were removed from the schoolŐs registry. When the decision was reversed, you were re-enrolled as a new student.Ó ŇAnd that means what for me? I have to take all of my old tests again?Ó ŇI should hope not. The thought of having to grade your second year Transfiguration work is a nightmare IŐd rather not relive,Ó McGonagall said, giving Harry a slight smile. ŇNo, it means that as of right now, you are no longer in Gryffindor.Ó ŇSoÉ put me back in Gryffindor?Ó ŇWe are confident that will indeed be the result. However, until the sorting hat officially places you back in Gryffindor, the magic of the school will not tie the award or loss of points to your house.Ó Harry tensed up at the thought of the sorting hat looking into his head for a third time. He tried not to let his trepidation show, so he shrugged instead. ŇSo what? Me not losing points anymore would probably help Gryffindor, soÉÓ ŇThis includes any points earned from Quidditch.Ó ŇOh.Ó ŇSo we will need to have you officially placed back in your house at the sorting ceremony. We have decided that you will be treated as a transfer student, so you will be sorted after the first years.Ó Her words finally started really sinking in with Harry. He was going to be sorted again. ŇUm, Professor?Ó he asked. ŇWhat happens if the hat puts meÉ somewhere other than Gryffindor?Ó ŇAs I mentioned earlier, we think itŐs highly unlikely that you will be placed elsewhere,Ó she said. ŇHowever, if it does, you will simply join your new house.Ó Harry wasnŐt nearly as confident that he was going straight back to Gryffindor, but he didnŐt say so. ŇDo you have any questions, Potter?Ó HarryŐs mind raced, and he kept finding his thoughts returning to the sorting hatŐs words to him in both his first and second years. The blasted hat wanted him in Slytherin then, and Harry didnŐt think anything had happened to change its mind. Especially since Harry had this shiny, new fascination with the dark arts, and that new determination to Ôuse any meansŐ to defeat VoldemortÉ hadnŐt the sorting hat said exactly that about Slytherins? And hadnŐt he learned in his reading that Slytherins were indeed more invested in the dark arts than any other Hogwarts house? Crap, Harry thought. IŐm definitely going to Slytherin this time. ŇHarry?Ó Sirius said quietly from the back of the room. Harry had honestly forgotten he was there. He realized that McGonagall is still waiting for his response. ŇI donŐt really have any questions, Professor,Ó he said. ŇAt least not now. ExceptÉ can I at least sit with Gryffindor until myÉ re-sorting? IŐd like to explain to them whatŐs happening. Just in case.Ó ŇUnderstandable,Ó McGonagall replied. ŇAnd yes, you may.Ó ŇHarry, thereŐs nothing to worry about,Ó Sirius said. ŇBoth of your parents were Gryffindors, and youŐre as Gryffindor as they come. ThereŐs no way youŐre going anywhere else.Ó *** Hermione and Ron seemed to be just as confident as Sirius that Harry would go straight back to Gryffindor. Fred and George treated it as a joke, saying that Harry could potentially get them business for WeasleyŐs Wizarding Wheezes in houses they didnŐt have access to. Mr. and Mrs. Weasley agreed with the others, pointing out that both of his parents had been in Gryffindor. Harry began to doubt his certainty of winding up in Slytherin. Even so, he turned back to the Black Library and started looking up ways to ward his bed and belongings. Just in case. Despite everyone elseŐs confidence that Harry would go back to Gryffindor, he could potentially wind up sleeping in the same room as the children of Death Eaters, after all. He supposed there was a slight chance of Ravenclaw, what with how much heŐd been studying, but he didnŐt think Ravenclaws would approve of him only being especially studious in only one subject. All the while something else wriggled around in the back of HarryŐs mind - something he wasnŐt yet entirely prepared to face head-on. Even so, his mind kept circling back to it: his newfound fascination with the dark arts. Harry had accepted that he was too interested and invested in the dark arts to stop his new path of research. He absolutely knew it should alarm him, and that he should ask Sirius about how far could be too far. But Harry didnŐt want to run the risk of Sirius telling him to stop when all he wanted was to learn more. Unlike the subjects he studied at school, which mostly felt like a chore, he wanted to soak up all of the dark arts knowledge he possibly could. All that led Harry to his current dilemma. It wasnŐt just that Harry was worried that his new interest would make him a shoo-in for Slytherin; he was also worried about how he could continue learning about the dark arts while he was in Gryffindor. He would have to hide his interest in Gryffindor, whereas Slytherin might actually encourage him in his new studies. Slytherin, of all houses, could actually wind up being useful to him, and that was making Harry not completely dread the fact that he could wind up among the snakes. He could learn from them. That was the thought that scared Harry the most; he should have completely and absolutely dreaded the mere thought of going to Slytherin. He should certainly not have been almost intrigued by the idea. But despite himself, it was intriguing him. Even though he had nothing to confirm that any of the Slytherins like Malfoy or Parkinson were into dark arts, he had enough circumstantial evidence to support his theory. And although he despised those Slytherins, he couldnŐt help but wonder if they could pass him knowledge about the dark arts that he couldnŐt gain from the Black Library. The timing of everything seemed almost auspicious. HeŐd started learning about the dark arts almost by accident mere days before McGonagall told him of his need to be sorted again. HarryŐs entire life - everything heŐd built since coming to the wizarding world - would change if he went to Slytherin. HeŐd likely lose his friends, and would probably have to watch his own back even more than he had in prior years because he wouldnŐt be among people he trusted. Even Sirius, who seemed to be accepting and even a little encouraging of HarryŐs new field of study, had expressed his dislike of Slytherin more than once. He loved Gryffindor. Despite his recent irritation, he loved his friends. He loved what the house stood for. He didnŐt want to lose any of the ragtag family heŐd gained since entering the wizarding world. The hat lets you choose, Harry desperately reminded himself. It takes your desire into account. I just have to want Gryffindor enough . *** Aside from Sirius making an unannounced trip with them to the station, the train ride to Hogwarts was, thankfully, relatively uneventful, although awkward. Ron and Hermione quickly abandoned him for the prefect meeting, and he found himself in a car with Ginny, Neville, and an odd Ravenclaw named Luna Lovegood. Ginny was the only other person in the train car that knew of HarryŐs imminent sorting, and she didnŐt mention it. Harry silently thanked her. Harry distracted himself with LunaŐs strange magazine called ÔThe Quibbler,Ő and he was amused to see the article on Sirius. It was relatively peaceful until Ron and Hermione joined them after their prefects meeting. Of course, Draco Malfoy appeared soon after, and everything about HarryŐs re-sorting came rushing back to him. I canŐt possibly wind up in the same house as him, Harry thought, staring at Malfoy in barely-concealed horror. ŇWhat are you looking at, Potter?Ó Draco demanded, snapping him out of his stupor. ŇNothing pleasant,Ó Harry muttered, wishing he could sink into his seat. ŇManners, Potter,Ó Malfoy said with a smirk. ŇOr IŐll have to give you detention -Ó ŇGood lord, Malfoy,Ó Harry said. ŇI am so not in the mood for this right now. Please go away.Ó Malfoy, as he expected, smirked even wider. ŇDid I touch a nerve? Not used to being second-best to -Ó Hermione stood up. ŇJust get out!Ó she shouted. To HarryŐs amazement, Draco departed, and Hermione slammed the door after him. ŇDid you really just say ÔpleaseŐ to Malfoy, Harry?Ó Ron said, his mouth full of a chocolate frog. Harry faced the window and rolled his eyes. The now-familiar prickling in his scar had returned, and he desperately wished he could just be alone for the rest of the ride. *** ŇWhereŐs your uniform, Harry?Ó Dean asked as soon as Harry sat down. HarryŐs black cloak wasnŐt adorned with the Gryffindor crest like the others. ŇUm.Ó Harry had specifically asked to sit with the Gryffindors prior to his sorting so he could explain what was going on, but now that he was here he had absolutely no idea what to say or how to even bring it up. ŇWill they just reset itÉ after?Ó Hermione asked, and Harry feels a wave of gratefulness towards her. He knew she could tell how uncomfortable he was, and she was trying to give him a window to ease into the conversation. ŇAfter what?Ó Dean asked. ŇAfter I getÉ sorted,Ó Harry said. ŇAgain.Ó Harry, with interjections from Hermione, explained to the other fifth years why and how he had to be sorted after the first years. ŇBut youŐll come right back here,Ó Ron said, somewhat unhelpfully. ŇHopefully,Ó Harry said. ŇI mean, itŐs not like itŐs guaranteed.Ó ŇWhere else would you go, Harry?Ó Dean asked. ŇSlytherin?Ó He started laughing, and Ron and a few others joined him. Harry didnŐt, and he noticed that Seamus didnŐt, either. The sorting of the first years seemed to go by faster than it ever had before, and then Dumbledore stood up. ŇWe had a series of unusual circumstances occur over this past summer, which has resulted in one of our older students needing to be sorted again,Ó Dumbledore said. ŇWe expect this is likely just a formality, but nevertheless, it needs to be done.Ó He paused for just a moment. ŇHarry Potter?Ó Harry braced himself and stood up, and then he heard Pansy Parkinson shriek with laughter from the Slytherin table. ŇDid Potter flunk himself all the way back to first year?Ó Perfect, Harry thought. Keep up that heckling; that way I wonŐt even be remotely tempted into wanting to be in the same house as you. The hat seemed to loom even larger than it had when he was a small first year, but when he sat down and McGonagall placed the hat on his head, the rim of the hat no longer slipped over his eyes. That left him able to clearly see every person in the Great Hall watching him with interest, so Harry closed his eyes. He didnŐt want to see them looking at him. ŇMr. Potter,Ó a familiar voice said in his ear. ŇIt is so rare that I get to speak with a student twice, much less three times, and itŐs even rarer that I actually get to sort a student more than once. You are a tricky one.Ó Gryffindor, please, Harry thought as hard as he could. ŇNow, then, Mr. Potter,Ó the hat said. ŇIt is my job to place students where they will thrive and be happy - which is why I will listen to a studentŐs desire. However, I would like you to listen to me before I place you anywhere.Ó Harry swallowed. ŇDespite your insistence on Gryffindor, you donŐt have nearly as much aversion to Slytherin as you did in your first year. I would be in danger. ŇOnly as much danger as you put yourself in every year,Ó the hat replied. ŇThe students themselves pose no danger. ŇYou need to learn how to think like a Slytherin: how to use every tool at your disposal, and how to survive whatŐs thrown at you.Ó I donŐt want to think like a Slytherin, Harry thought desperately. TheyŐve been nothing but cruel to me and my friends. ŇThose are not the defining traits of a Slytherin, though,Ó the hat said in reply. ŇAnd the Slytherins of recent years have been cruel more as a reaction to outside forces, rather than as an inherent part of their Slytherin traits.Ó That comment just confused Harry, so he decided to try a different tactic. IŐd lose all of my friends if I went to Slytherin. ŇThat would be entirely up to you and them,Ó the hat said. ŇYou can be friends with those outside of your own house, after all. And who's to say you wouldn't make friends in a new house?Ó The hat paused. ŇBut I fear weŐre getting off track. ŇSlytherin has more to offer you than Gryffindor - that much is a fact. I want to be able to defend the people heŐs attacking. I donŐt want anyone else to die. ŇUnfortunately, I think people dying in the upcoming war will be inevitable,Ó the hat said. ŇBut with one track you may be able to lessen the blow, and you may be able to save more.Ó Then thatŐs the one I want, Harry said. Gryffindors defend others. TheyŐre brave and will stand up to anything. That's where I belong. The hat chuckled softly. ŇBut just imagine what a Slytherin with the heart of a Gryffindor could accomplish?Ó The hat fell silent for a moment, and Harry realized that he could hear a growing crowd of whispers in the Great Hall. People wondered what could possibly be taking so long. Harry opened his eyes and to see that everyone still stared at him, and he shifted uncomfortably on the stool. ŇLet them stare,Ó the hat said. ŇThey will always stare, no matter where you wind up. You must answer me now - will you allow me to place you where you can accomplish what you wish to accomplish?Ó Ever since McGonagall had come and spoken to him, Harry had imagined a thousand different conversations he could have had with the sorting hat, and none of them had gone the way this one had. HeŐd been determined to think of nothing but how much he wanted to go to Gryffindor, and instead the blasted hat made him doubt everything. HeŐd started looking into dark arts because he wanted to defeat Voldemort. He wanted to stay with Gryffindor in order to save others. Even though the end goal was essentially the same, the two were clearly not compatible with one another, but Harry didnŐt think heŐd be willing to give up one for the other. Gryffindor wouldnŐt allow him to continue researching the dark arts, and his continued study would surely get him in trouble in his old house. ŇMr. Potter, you are close to becoming a hatstall. Will you allow me to place you or not?Ó Harry finally realized exactly what the hat was asking him, and he realized that he couldnŐt delay any longer. He closed his eyes again. Put me where you think I belong, he thought in resignation. Harry swore he could actually hear the hat smile in satisfaction, and a sudden wave of regret swept through him. A split-second decision made under pressure was going to determine his last few years at Hogwarts; what if it was the wrong one? No, he thought. Wait ."Too late, Mr. Potter,Ó the hat said. ŇDespite your hesitation, itŐs clear that you know exactly where you belong, and itŐs been clear to me from day one that you belong in... SLYTHERIN!Ó Chapter2:The Covenant Chapter Notes The Trevor Project and Trans Lifeline are two wonderful organizations that provide great services for trans people. Thank you! ŇToo late, Mr. Potter,Ó the hat said. ŇDespite your hesitation, itŐs clear that you know exactly where you belong, and itŐs been clear to me from day one that you belong in... SLYTHERIN!Ó The last word was announced to the hall, and several things seemed to happen all at once. The hat was torn off his head and McGonagall stared down at him just as Harry heard a crash of glass behind him at the head table. He heard gasping across the entire hall, and Ron yelled, ŇWhat?!Ó at the same time that Parkinson let out a shriek from the Slytherin table. ŇDid the hat just - did it really just say that Potter is one of us now?Ó she said, obviously to her housemates, but loud enough that Harry could hear it clear as a bell. Harry turned and craned his neck toward the head table, where it appeared that Snape was the one who knocked over his goblet. Shit. Snape, who had stood up and started marching toward him, was now HarryŐs head-of-fucking-house. Holy shit. ŇMr. Potter,Ó McGonagall said. ŇThis did not turn out as anyone expected, but I must say that itŐs been a pleasure to have you in my house, and you will be missed.Ó She visibly schooled her shocked expression as she began rolling up the roster scroll in her hands. ŇMy door will be open should you wish to talk.Ó ŇYes, maŐam,Ó Harry said, almost robotically. ŇThank you.Ó He didnŐt want to look at the Gryffindor table, afraid of what he might see. Harry heard the growing roar of whispers in the crowd suddenly fall silent, and he realized that Snape must have cast a muffling charm. ŇPotter,Ó he said, the vehemence dripping from his voice. ŇHow and why are you now in my house?Ó Harry wasnŐt entirely sure how to answer that. ŇThe hat put me here.Ó Harry winced. ŇSorry,Ó he said. Despite the history between himself and Snape, he didnŐt want to start out his time in Slytherin on a bad note, and Harry knew Snape well enough to know that heŐd find his response to be sarcastic, even though he hadnŐt been intending it that way. ŇSorry, sir,Ó he corrected. ŇIt is obvious that the hat put you in my house,Ó Snape said. ŇI was present when the hat deigned to do so. ŇIt wanted to put me in Slytherin in first year,Ó he said carefully. ŇI guessÉ I guess it got its way this time.Ó SnapeŐs expression changed, but Harry couldnŐt exactly read what that new expression meant. ŇWith me, Potter,Ó he said, gesturing for Harry to follow him. Snape began to lead Harry to the Slytherin table, and all of HarryŐs defenses immediately rose. For the next three years, he was going to be in the same house as the boy who had lived to make his life a living hell for the previous four years. Goddamn fucking manipulative sorting hat. ŇSlytherins,Ó Snape said. ŇI realize that this recent addition to our house turn may come as a shock.Ó No fucking kidding, Harry thought. ŇI am well aware that some of you may have a turbulent history with your new housemate,Ó Snape continued. ŇI want to make it clear that despite any conflicts that may have arisen in the past, Mr. Potter is now a Slytherin.Ó And there it was. Harry was a Slytherin. ŇIt should be noted that apparently, the hat wanted to place him in Slytherin from the beginning, and how did you put it, Potter? This time it Ôgot its way?ŐÓ ŇYes,Ó Harry said quietly. ŇSir.Ó Snape had the nerve to smirk. ŇThe hat has ultimately decided that Potter shall spend his last three years in Slytherin. ŇThis includes you, Potter.Ó ŇYes, sir.Ó ŇTake your seat.Ó Two Slytherins - Zabini and Greengrass, he believed - slid apart to make a space for Harry. ŇI really should be going, Harry; I feel like IŐm shirking my responsibilities as prefect. Can we meet up tomorrow?Ó Harry nodded. ŇI havenŐt gotten the Slytherin time-table yet, but yes. ŇPlease be careful, Harry.Ó ŇI will.Ó They parted ways, and Harry turned towards the Slytherin table, where it wasnŐt just Zabini and Greengrass waiting for him, but the rest of the Slytherin fifth years, minus the prefects. They had evidently been talking, and most likely about him, because they all shut their mouths as he made his way back towards them. *** Harry still wasnŐt sure if he should be expecting a fight in the common room, so he braced himself as Greengrass gave the password - ÔVeritaserumŐ - to the door. He walked into what appeared to be the end of a speech Parkinson and Malfoy were giving to the first years, explaining the rules, and where everything was. Harry vaguely wondered if heŐd get the same spiel, or if heŐd just have to figure it out on his own. The Slytherins seemed to disperse to different corners of the common room, and Harry began studying it. HeŐd been here once before, of course, but he now noticed just how different it was from Gryffindor tower. Moonlight refracted through the lake to the windows, which cast a green glow over everything. The couches looked only moderately more welcoming than they had before, and there were circular tables and chairs evidently set up for study. A few paintings hung on the walls, including one of a sleeping green snake overlooking one of the study areas. A little on the nose, Harry thought. ŇCŐmon, Potter,Ó Zabini said. ŇFifth year meeting.Ó He started walking towards a group of couches, where Crabbe and Goyle already sat, and another boy - Nott - was pulling up a chair. Part of Harry wanted to decline, but he reluctantly followed. ŇIs this so you can explain 'what being a Slytherin means?'Ó His voice was more sarcastic than he'd been intending; Zabini had been rather pleasant to him so far, after all. Zabini laughed. ŇThatŐs part of it,Ó he said. ŇBut our year has a tradition of getting together for a meeting one night a week. Since you are now in our year, you are taking part.Ó He didnŐt phrase it as if Harry had a choice. Parkinson and Malfoy were also making their way over. Harry stood in front of the other fifth years, feeling awkward, before Zabini pulled up a chair for him. ŇSit, Potter.Ó Harry sat as the rest of the fifth years gathered, and Harry looked at each of their faces, trying to see them as his housemates. Instead of Hermione, Lavender, Parvati, and the other Gryffindor girls, there were Millicent Bulstrode, Tracey Davis, Daphne Greengrass, and Pansy Parkinson. The Slytherin boys were comprised of Blaise Zabini, Vincent Crabbe, Gregory Goyle, Theodore Nott, and, of course, Draco Malfoy. Draco Fucking Malfoy. Draco Malfoy, of course, was the son of Lucius Malfoy. Nott, Crabbe, and Goyle, were also all sons of Death Eaters. Those Death Eaters had been in the graveyard with Voldemort the previous spring, laughing as Voldemort tried to kill Harry. Not for the first time that night, Harry found himself wondering how the hell he wound up in the same house as the children of Death Eaters. HeŐd known it was a possibility, of course, but the reality of it seemed far stranger than heŐd imagined. Now he actually sat with Malfoy and the rest, about to take part in what was apparently a weekly Slytherin plotting meeting. Harry shifted uncomfortably in his chair while Zabini, by contrast, relaxed in the chair next to him, looking almost elegant as he crossed his foot across his knee. After all of the fifth years sat down, Harry realized that they all looked just as awkward as he felt. ŇWell,Ó Parkinson finally said, breaking the silence. ŇI, for one, cannot wait to see Potter in a Slytherin uniform.Ó Harry stared at her in disbelief, and she smirked back at him. ŇThe Gryffindor red clashed with your coloring,Ó she said. ŇSlytherin green will bring out your eyes.Ó Greengrass let out a giggle. ŇAnd that, Potter, is Pansy,Ó she said. ŇSheŐll spin fashion into it somehow.Ó ŇBut something really must be done about those glasses,Ó Parkinson continued. ŇMagically fixing your eyesight would be far preferable, but at the very least you need something more... distinguished.Ó ŇIs thatÉ possible?Ó Harry asked. ŇFixing my eyesight?Ó HeŐd never even considered that magic could accomplish that; after all, why would any wizard wear glasses if they could just magically fix their eyes? ŇItŐs possible, yes,Ó Malfoy said. ŇIt just costs several hundred galleons, so not many can afford it.Ó And there was the snooty Malfoy that Harry was familiar with. It was almost a comfort. ŇIŐllÉ look into it, I guess,Ó Harry said, unsure. ŇI guess it would be useful for Qui-Ó He cut himself off. Quidditch was the word that had nearly come out of his mouth, but the state of him playing Quidditch hadnŐt even occurred to him until just then. But of course, Malfoy seemed to know exactly what heŐd been about to say. ŇWhat are you going to do about Quidditch?Ó Harry sighed. ŇConsidering all this just happened, like, an hour ago,Ó he said dryly, "I havenŐt had much time to think about it.Ó ŇYouŐll be playing Seeker for Slytherin, of course.Ó Harry froze. ŇWhat?Ó Malfoy sneered. ŇDo you have a hearing problem in addition to a problem with your eyesight? You will play Seeker.Ó ŇButÉ youŐre SlytherinŐs Seeker.Ó ŇYouŐre better than me,Ó Malfoy said. Harry sputtered. ŇDid youÉ did you just compliment me?Ó Holy shit. Malfoy shrugged. ŇItŐs merely simple facts. Besides, IŐm more than a fair chaser,Ó he said, and then he grinned. ŇWith you on Seeker and me on Chaser, Slytherin will likely go undefeated, and we will almost certainly win the Quidditch Cup.Ó Harry felt like heŐd entered an alternate dimension, but he considered MalfoyŐs words. ŇWellÉ I meanÉ I like winning.Ó Most of the Slytherins around him let out a laugh, Zabini loudest of all of them. ŇThatŐs the most Slytherin thing youŐve said so far,Ó Zabini said. ŇUhÉ thank you? At least none of us seem to know what to say, Harry thought. ŇSoÉÓ Harry finally said. ŇWhat are these rules or whatever you were talking about? Or was that just a joke?Ó ŇNo joke,Ó Zabini said. ŇItŐs why we wanted to sit down tonight,Ó Parkinson said. ŇWe normally wait until Fridays, but with you joining our houseÉÓ Harry raised an eyebrow. He started to feel like heŐd been pulled into an intervention. ŇYouŐre one of us, now, Potter,Ó Malfoy said. ŇItŐs beyond bizarre, but you are a Slytherin.Ó ŇI donŐt needÉ reminding,Ó Harry said. ŇSee that you donŐt,Ó Malfoy said haughtily. ŇDraco, stop,Ó Parkinson said, lightly swatting MalfoyŐs arm. ŇWhy donŐt you just let me know what this Slytherin ÔcodeŐ is?Ó Harry asked. "Don't call it anything so plebeian as a 'code,'" Draco said, his lip curling. "It's more like a covenant that we uphold through all of our actions," Greengrass added. Zabini uncrossed his legs and leaned forward. ŇThereŐs quite a bit to it,Ó he said. ŇBut it all amounts to this: Slytherins protect one another.Ó In Slytherin, youŐll make your real friends... Harry raised an eyebrow. ŇI meanÉ so do Gryffindors.Ó ŇNot like us,Ó Malfoy spat. ŇWeŐve seen how some Gryffindors have turned on you in the past. YouŐll never get that from us.Ó ŇAt least not in public, right?Ó Harry said, remembering MalfoyŐs comment at dinner. ŇRight.Ó Malfoy shot Harry a wolfish grin. ŇItŐs more than that, though,Ó Parkinson said. ŇIt means we look out for one another in the halls. We help each other out when someone needs it, because the success of one Slytherin helps all Slytherins.Ó ŇAnd give each other free fashion advice?Ó Harry said sardonically and was surprised when Parkinson laughed. ŇRight. We donŐt want our house looking shabby.Ó ŇWe donŐt turn each other in,Ó Nott said quietly, and Harry looked over at him in surprise. It was the first thing heŐd heard from the boy since Harry had been sorted into their house. The other Slytherins were nodding in agreement. ŇNo matter what you see, no matter what you hear - you will never report it to a teacher,Ó Malfoy said. Harry could read between the lines, and he instantly realized what they were talking about. ItŐs why you werenŐt completely against being sorted here, Harry reminded himself. TheyŐre talking about dark arts. However, Harry realized that it could also be about something else - like expressing loyalties to a certain dark lord. Harry gnawed on the inside of his cheek, wondering if they would just blatantly say what they meant, so he could say right back to them that heŐd also been neck-deep in researching illegal magic. Harry mulled over the idea of just broaching the topic himself, but he also wasnŐt about to start trusting the people in that house on his first night, especially when he knew for a fact that some of them had parents that followed Voldemort, and some had expressed ideals that fell in line with Voldemort. No, he needed to wait for proof. He needed to have something on them before he trusted them with his own secret. That also sounds very Slytherin of me, Harry thought. Instead, Harry shrugged. ŇWhenever IŐve reported something to a teacher in the past, they never believed me. I gave up on all that a long time ago.Ó The Slytherins didnŐt exactly seem satisfied with that answer, but no one commented, so Harry tried again, wondering if he could coax them into being more comfortable with him. ŇJust out of curiosity, would thatÉ part of the 'covenant' go both ways?Ó Harry asked. ŇIf you caught me doing something against the rules, what would you do?Ó ŇWeŐd tell you not to get caught,Ó Zabini said. ŇPlanning on more nighttime excursions, Potter?Ó Malfoy said, a bite in his tone. Harry narrowed his eyes. It wasnŐt the angle heŐd been intending on, but he supposed it was still a valid question for him. ŇWhen it suits me,Ó he replied. Zabini laughed. ŇSee, guys - there is Slytherin in him.Ó ŇPotter,Ó Nott said suddenly. ŇI really want to ask you something, but I think itŐs going to make you angry.Ó ŇTheo,Ó Goyle said, a warning tone in his voice. ŇOh, come on, Greg,Ó Nott said. ŇNo,Ó Malfoy said, his eyes dark. ŇDonŐt get me wrong, Theo - I want to know, too. But I donŐt think now is the time to be asking that.Ó ŇAsking what?Ó Harry asked, curious. ŇNot now. ŇYour Ôidiot Muggle relatives?ŐÓ MalfoyŐs expression immediately lightened up. ŇYes! No offense, Potter, but I had you pegged for aÉ Muggle lover.Ó ŇIt must be awful, having to live with Muggles,Ó Zabini added. Harry was simultaneously annoyed and bewildered. They were spouting the usual anti-Muggle nonsense, but they were doing it in a way that made it sound like they were trying to be sympathetic with Harry. Harry knew navigating the odd landscape of wizarding superiority and blood purity ideologies in Slytherin would be difficult, and they were making it even more difficult. ŇIŐm notÉ anti-Muggle,Ó Harry said slowly, his eyes narrowing. Despite wanting to at least try to get along with the members of his house, he didnŐt want that rumor spreading. ŇMy relatives are justÉÓ He sighed. ŇMost Muggles arenŐt like them.Ó Despite his dislike of the Dursleys, Harry found that he truly regretted making that throw-away comment at dinner. ŇWhat are they like?Ó Davis asked. ŇTheyŐreÉÓ Harry sighed. ŇI hate saying this, but theyŐd probably confirm every single anti-Muggle sentiment all of you have.Ó ŇHey!Ó Greengrass suddenly snapped, breaking her silence. ŇDonŐt assume weŐre all anti-Muggle. I take Muggle studies because I think the Muggles are impressive. I respect what theyŐve done, especially in the last century or so. Did you know theyŐve actually been to the moon?Ó Parkinson laughed. ŇPlease donŐt get started on Muggle science, Daphne,Ó she said. ŇLast time you were ranting about it for days.Ó ŇBut they landed on the moon !Ó Greengrass said again, throwing her hands up. ŇWizards have never done anything like that!Ó ŇWhy would we need to go to the moon?Ó Zabini asked dryly. Harry, meanwhile, stared at Greengrass. It seemed navigating the Slytherin beliefs would be even more difficult than heŐd imagined, but at least he wasnŐt the only person who didnŐt believe in wizarding superiority in the house. "My relatives are much, much stupider than astronauts," he finally said. ŇTheyŐre yourÉ motherŐs relatives, arenŐt they?Ó Davis asked. Harry nodded. ŇMy motherŐs sister, her husband, and her son - my cousin.Ó Harry rolled his head back to stare at the ceiling of the common room. ŇThereŐs... very little resemblance.Ó He really didnŐt want to be talking about this, especially with the Slytherins. ŇBut if theyŐre so awful,Ó Greengrass said, Ňwhy do you have to stay with them?Ó Harry coughed. ŇThere isnŐt a choice.Ó ŇFor you?Ó Malfoy said incredulously. ŇOf course there would be a choice!Ó Harry snapped his gaze to Malfoy. ŇThereŐs a lot to it,Ó he said. ŇCan we please talk about something else?Ó He turned his stare to Nott. ŇWhat was it you wanted to ask me earlier?Ó ŇLetŐs talk about something else, but not that, either,Ó Malfoy said. Another awkward silence fell. ŇCan I justÉ go check out the dorm?Ó Harry finally said. ŇThis has beenÉ a very strange day for me.Ó If Harry didnŐt know better, he would have said that ParkinsonŐs expression almost looked concerned. ŇOf course.Ó ŇOur dorm is in the left hall, third door on the right," Blaise said. Harry stood up and then turned back to the Slytherins. ŇIÉÓ He sighed. ŇThis is going to sound strange, but I appreciate the fact that youÉ that weŐre allÉÓ ŇI know our history, Potter,Ó Malfoy said. ŇProbably better than anyone else here. WeŐre taking SnapeŐs order seriously. WeŐll figure out why youŐre a Slytherin eventually. Until then, youŐre still going to be treated as one.Ó Parkinson abruptly sat up straight. ŇThatŐs right. Also, officially - as your prefects, you can come to us with any problems youŐre going to have.Ó Harry raised an eyebrow at that. ŇÔGoingŐ to have?Ó he repeated. ŇYouŐll see.Ó Chapter3:The Glasses Chapter Notes The Trevor Project and Trans Lifeline are two wonderful organizations that provide great services for trans people. Please consider giving them a visit to see how you can get involved! This story is an AU of "Order of the Phoenix," and although I will likely not directly copy any passages, there will be multiple references to events in that book. This chapter is a little heavy-handed with the references. Let me know if you spot them all. ;) 11/17/2019: 2nd beta pass by guestuser28653. Thank you! See the end of the chapter for more notes The Slytherin dorm had a different layout than Gryffindor tower. It was larger, for one, and it was rectangular rather than circular. Four sets of beds, nightstands, desks, and wardrobes rested on one side of the room, with two more sets on the other side. The extra space in the corner of the other side of the room was filled with a fireplace and a sofa, like a miniature version of the common room. The beds were identical to those in Gryffindor, though with dark green canopies and curtains rather than the familiar Gryffindor red. Harry was grateful to find his trunk at the foot of the bed closest to the door. At least he wouldnŐt feel trapped. Even so, he immediately went to his trunk and unlocked it with a tap of his wand. He first pulled out the knife he had tucked in a compartment under the lid and then dug to the bottom of his hastily packed belongings. A wave of relief went through him as he felt the spines of the dozen or so books heŐd brought with him from the Black Library. No one had found them and taken them away from him. It had been a risk bringing them with him, but he didnŐt think heŐd be able to find any similar material at Hogwarts. He blindly felt around for the smallest book in the stack and pulled it out, upending most of what had been packed on top of the books. Harry didnŐt pay any mind to the mess heŐd made and slammed his trunk shut as he flipped the book open to a page heŐd bookmarked. Just in case, Harry thought. Looks like I actually need it, after all. He placed the book, titled All The Wards YouŐll Ever Need, on top of his trunk and studied the page. He was fairly sure heŐd already memorized the whole thing, but he wanted to make sure. After all, heŐd tried basic curses and hexes, but he hadnŐt tried a blood ward yet. The entire spell looked very simple. It was a basic ward that was only supposed to allow him and any close relatives to access whatever he had warded. Anyone who wasnŐt of his blood that tried to access the warded object would be frozen in place until he let them out. Harry supposed that since he didnŐt have any living relatives aside from the Dursleys, he had little to worry about. Harry read through the page two more times before standing upright and walking to the side of his bed. He braced himself, and holding the knife in his right hand, he made a cut on his left index finger. He squeezed the finger a bit to allow blood to well up and then began to trace a symbol on his right hand. ŇFootot sanguinis.Ó He then knelt by his bed and lifted up the bedcover, uncovering the dark wood of the frame underneath. ŇLacquera! Ó he said as he slammed his bloody palm onto the frame. He actually saw the spell rise into place, with a dark purple-blue light originating at his palm and spreading over the entire bed before disappearing. Harry had to admit that he felt rather impressed with himself. He could tell that heŐd done the spell correctly and that the ward was in place; if he placed his hand near his bed he could actually feel the magic thrumming through it. He repeated the process on his trunk, even though it was already magically locked. If anyone tried to access his trunk, he wanted to know who it was. Harry grinned in satisfaction, and then looked back to the book. He hadnŐt studied the entire text yet; in fact, the only reason it had even come with him was because heŐd found that blood ward, and he had known he would need it if he were sorted into Slytherin. After a momentŐs consideration, Harry tucked the book under his pillow before opening his trunk to unpack. His school robes were all still unadorned; no Slytherin crest or colors had appeared yet. Harry remembered that back in first year his robes remained solid black until the morning after heŐd been sorted, so he supposed they would likely have green and silver accents by the morning. Unpacking didnŐt take him very long; he didnŐt have very many belongings. But when he uncovered the photo album Hagrid had given him in first year, he paused. He flipped through a few pages until he came to a photo of just his mother and father leaning up against a fence. They smiled up at him. ŇIŐm sorry,Ó Harry whispered. ŇI know this isnŐt where you wanted me to be.Ó He traced his motherŐs face with his thumb and she laughed, as if it tickled. In a split-second decision, Harry carefully removed the photo from the album and placed the book back in his trunk. He looked at the photo again. ŇIŐll try to make you proud, anyway,Ó he said, then placed the photo on his nightstand, leaning it up against the wall. He wanted to see their faces watching over him, but he also felt like he wanted to remind all of his roommates exactly who his parents had been. He changed into his nightclothes and got onto his bed. It might have been his imagination, but he could have sworn that his new bed was far more comfortable than the bed heŐd had in Gryffindor. Harry leaned back on his pillow and contemplated his day. All in all, it had gone a thousand times better than heŐd expected. He hadnŐt predicted that the Slytherins would actually try to play nice with him; he had truthfully expected to have to dodge attacks and insults from all sides. He wondered what Ron and Hermione were up to in Gryffindor tower, and what new concoctions Fred and George were unleashing upon their house. The thought made him ache, so he reached under his pillow for All The Wards YouŐll Ever Need as a distraction. He drew the curtains and read by wandlight until he fell asleep. *** Harry adjusted his new tie, drawing it up to his collar, and then stared at his reflection in the mirror on the inner door of his wardrobe. As he expected, his uniforms had been decorated with the Slytherin colors overnight .probably by house elves - but the effect of actually seeing himself in them wasnŐt something heŐd been expecting. He actually looked like a bloody Slytherin. His tie was green and silver, and his robes had green accents. His chest bore the Slytherin crest, and the snake on the crest seemed to approve. ŇYou look rather striking in those colors, if I might say,Ó the mirror said. Harry ignored the comment and just continued to stare. Zabini came into the room and let out a chuckle. ŇAre you ready yet, or do we have another Draco on our hands?Ó ŇWhat?Ó Harry yelped. Zabini grinned. "Staring at the mirror is one of Draco's favorite hobbies." Harry slammed his wardrobe shut. ŇNo, itŐs justÉÓ He looked down at himself and he awkwardly held up his arm to inspect the green accents on his sleeves. ŇThis is bizarre.Ó ŇYou look fine,Ó Zabini said. ŇShall we go to breakfast?Ó Harry nodded and followed Zabini into the common room, where they found Nott waiting in one of the chairs with half-lidded eyes. ŇUh, good morning,Ó Harry said. Nott grunted in response. Blaise laughed. ŇDonŐt bother talking to Theo for at least another twenty minutes,Ó he quipped. ŇHeŐs not a morning person. LetŐs go.Ó They traveled through the halls in a line. Harry tried to take up the back of the group, but not-a-morning-person Nott kept dragging his feet and Harry found himself following Zabini with Nott trailing behind. As they exited the Slytherin corridor, Harry saw a group of fourth-year Hufflepuffs coming up the adjacent hallway. The Hufflepuffs stopped when they saw the Slytherins, and Harry couldnŐt help but feel that they were staring at him. ŇCome on, Theo,Ó Zabini complained, ignoring the Hufflepuffs. ŇAt the rate weŐre going Pansy and Draco are actually going to beat us there.Ó They began to ascend the stairs to the great hall, and more and more students began to appear. Harry was now certain that they were staring at him. It wasnŐt as if they were being shy about it, after all. They finally reached the hall and out of force of habit, Harry immediately looked towards the Gryffindor table. Ron and Hermione were nowhere in sight, but NevilleŐs mouth fell open when he spotted Harry sandwiched in between Zabini and Nott. Harry smiled weakly and gave Neville a slight wave. Neville appeared flabbergasted, but he waved back hesitantly. A wave of appreciation ran through Harry. Maybe Hermione wouldnŐt be his only friend that remained in Gryffindor. He didnŐt want to face the possibility that he could lose his friendship with Ron. He hadnŐt even been a Slytherin for a full day yet; perhaps it would still work out. As they walked across the hall, Harry could see more and more heads turning towards him. He stopped looking at the tables as they passed them, instead studiously focusing his eyes on a spot on ZabiniŐs neck. When they reached the Slytherin table, Harry took the same seat heŐd had at the feast the previous night and began filling his plate. While Nott remained silent, Zabini tried to make small talk with Harry. ŇIŐm curious to see our fifth year time tables. All the upper years have said that the jump in coursework between fourth and fifth year is like night and day.Ó Harry nodded, feeling rather dumb. ŇYeah.Ó ŇWhat electives do you take?Ó ŇCare of Magical Creatures and Divination,Ó Harry replied. ŇYou?Ó ŇArithmancy and Ancient Runes.Ó ŇIŐve heard those are difficult.Ó Zabini laughed. ŇThe hat was torn between Ravenclaw and Slytherin for me, remember? I like the challenge. Besides, those subjects are useful.Ó Harry smiled weakly. ŇWhatŐs Divination like? I donŐt think any Slytherins in our year take it.Ó Harry rolled his eyes. ŇItŐs a joke,Ó he said. ŇAs long as I make up a prediction about my horrible and grizzly death, Trewlaney gives me at least an E.Ó He took a bite of his eggs. ŇIf you dare to predict anything happy, sheŐll fail you.Ó Zabini raised an eyebrow. ŇThat sounds -Ó A shriek cut him off. ŇI knew it!Ó Parkinson and Malfoy were approaching the table, and Parkinson ran - actually ran - to the side where Harry and Zabini were sitting. ŇStand up! Let me look at you!Ó Parkinson demanded. She started tugging on HarryŐs shoulder. "Uh, I've already had enough of being looked at today," Harry said in feeble protest. "I don't care. Up!" Parkinson yanked on Harry's elbow so hard that he began to tilt backward over the bench, and he had to throw his legs over and get to his feet in order to prevent himself from falling on his skull. "What the bloody, buggering fuck -" "Such eloquence, Potter," Malfoy said from the other side of the table. Harry shot him a glare. Parkinson grabbed Harry's shoulder and rotated him so he was facing her. She looked him up and down with an appraising look. "Give us a turn, please." "What?" "Just do as she asks, Potter," Malfoy said. "She won't leave you alone until you do." "Turn!" Parkinson commanded, and she put both hands on Harry's shoulders to spin him around. "Why am I doing this?" Harry asked. He wasn't sure if he was asking why he was twirling, or why he was allowing Parkinson to manhandle him so bodily. "Harry Potter," Parkinson said after she had gotten Harry to complete a full rotation. "You almost look like a proper Slytherin." "ŐAlmost?Ő" Harry raised an eyebrow. HeŐd thought he looked pretty damn Slytherin in the mirror. "Those glasses, thoughÉ" Parkinson frowned. "They have to go." Without warning, she reached out and snatched Harry's glasses right off of his face. "Hey!" Harry snapped. HeŐd been trying to play nice with his new housemates, but Parkinson was quickly pushing him to his limit. "You have brilliant eyes," Parkinson said, ignoring HarryŐs protest. "You shouldn't hide them behind those horrendous glasses." "I need those horrendous glasses to see," Harry said flatly to the vaguely Parkinson-shaped blob in front of him. "The hair might be hopeless, though," he heard Malfoy say, and Harry rolled his eyes. "I think the hair works, actually," Parkinson said. "ItŐs an uncontrolled billywig nest right now, but just a touch of product will turn it into Ôjust-rolled-out-of-bedŐ tousled.Ó Harry heard Zabini start laughing. "Is that an official style, Pans?" "Yes." Parkinson's voice made it clear that she was not to be argued with. Harry's glasses were abruptly jammed back onto his face. He glared at Parkinson, but movement over by the hall's entrance caught his eye. Ron and Hermione stood there. They had apparently been watching the spectacle. When Ron noticed Harry looking at them, he grabbed Hermione by the arm and began pulling her towards the Gryffindor table. Hermione didn't resist. Harry sighed and took his seat again. A few minutes later, Professor Snape came around with their time-tables. At first, Harry was relieved to see that his day would start off with Herbology, but then saw that it was immediately followed by double Potions. He thankfully didn't have to face Umbridge until the following day. ŇHerbology followed by Potions,Ó Malfoy said. ŇThank goodness for Professor Snape. He clearly did that on purpose; Herbology is complementary to Potions.Ó Harry sighed again. His first Potions class as a Slytherin - which also happened to be his first class with the Gryffindors as a Slytherin - was sure to be eventful. *** Herbology was not eventful. It was, thankfully, with the Ravenclaws, who seemed more interested in learning the material than spending their time staring at Harry. Professor Sprout began the class with an unusually stern lecture - stern for her, anyway .about how it was the O.W.L. year and that it was high time to actually buckle down and get serious. It wasnŐt bad, aside from that. He partnered with Zabini, and the class went by without a hitch. They walked as a group to Potions. Despite himself, Harry found himself curious about how the class would go. Professor Snape had been awful to Harry since his first year, assigning detention and detracting points from Harry right and left, but he had also always favored the Slytherins. Now that Harry was a Slytherin, he realized he had no idea what to expect from Snape. IŐve never seen him take points from Slytherin, Harry realized, and he suddenly found himself marginally optimistic. They filed into the Potions classroom and Harry started to make his way over to Ron and Hermione; they had occupied the same table together for years, and he was eager to try and talk to Ron. He sat down and opened his mouth to greet them. ŇPotter,Ó Snape suddenly said from behind him. Harry couldnŐt help it - he jumped. ŇUntil you grow adjusted to your recentÉ change of residence, you will sit with members of your own house.Ó Harry stared at Snape and contemplated arguing; this was, after all, one of the only chances he would have to spend time with his friends. Across the classroom, the Slytherins had frozen in place, and then suddenly, amazingly, they began adjusting their seating arrangements to make room for him. They didnŐt speak while they moved, but in some odd sort of silent communication, Crabbe and Goyle moved back two tables while Parkinson moved up to the table behind Malfoy. Zabini joined Malfoy on the left, leaving the seat to MalfoyŐs right open. Perfect, Harry thought unhappily. At least Zabini was there, as well - heŐd been the most pleasant Slytherin thus far. Harry got up with a silent sigh and took the seat next to Malfoy. Snape, like Professor Sprout, opened his lesson with a lecture about their O.W.L.s, ending it with a threat that anyone who didnŐt receive an ÔOŐ on their Potions O.W.L. would not be continuing to N.E.W.T. level courses. The speech was far more intimidating than SproutŐs, and Harry sincerely hoped he would do well enough; he still thought being an auror was appealing, and he needed the Potions N.E.W.T. to accomplish that. When Snape finally moved on to the practical, he revealed they would be brewing something called ÔThe Draught of Peace,Ő a potion that evidently came up quite often in O.W.L. exams. Without explaining it, he pointed to directions on the blackboard, opened the ingredient cabinet with a wave of his wand, and informed them all that they had an hour and a half to complete the potion. Harry stared at the directions and sighed. Snape had set them with the most finicky potion Harry had ever seen. Beside him, Malfoy was diligently writing on a piece of parchment. He kept looking up at the blackboard and then back down at his writing. Harry shrugged and went to retrieve his ingredients. The potion was indeed ridiculous. It had to be stirred an exact number of times, sometimes clockwise, sometimes counterclockwise. The temperature had to be adjusted. The timing of every step had to be perfect, and the ingredients had to be added in a precise order. An hour later, most of the class was nearly done, Harry included. He grabbed his wand and prepared to adjust the flame under his potion. Suddenly, a hand grasped his wrist and pulled it away from his cauldron. Harry looked up, startled. Malfoy released his wrist and pointed at the blackboard. Harry glanced up and had to squint to see through the vapors, and he struggled to read the writing. Malfoy let out an overly dramatic, exasperated sigh and shoved his parchment in between them, tapping his finger on a line heŐd written in elegant script. Harry read the line Malfoy was pointing at and realized that heŐd nearly forgotten to add the syrup of hellebore. In other words, Malfoy had just saved HarryŐs potion. Harry didnŐt want to speak out loud in SnapeŐs quiet classroom, but he nodded at Malfoy in thanks. He added the hellebore, and then proceeded to use MalfoyŐs written instructions for the remainder of the potion. About ten minutes later, Snape proceeded to inspect everyoneŐs work. RonŐs potion was spitting and emitting a foul odor, and poor Neville looked like he was about to throw up at whatever resided in his cauldron. HermioneŐs, of course, looked perfect, and Harry was surprised to realize that his own potion appeared identical. Snape stopped at his and MalfoyŐs table, and Harry, by instinct, tensed up. ŇMr. Potter,Ó Snape said. Harry swallowed nervously and looked up at Snape. ŇI believe this may be the first time you have achieved such an... adequate result in my class,Ó Snape continued. ŇYou appear to have successfully brewed the Draught of Peace.Ó Then a smirk began to draw itself across SnapeŐs face, and a sinking feeling began to develop in his stomach. Whatever Snape was about to say, he knew from years of experience it wouldnŐt be pleasant. ŇTen points will be awarded toÉ Slytherin,Ó he said, apparently with much satisfaction, Ňfor heeding your classmateŐs advice.Ó HarryŐs mouth fell open. In all his years at Hogwarts, Snape had never awarded points to Harry. ŇWhat?Ó he exclaimed. Malfoy promptly stepped on his foot. ŇMr. Malfoy, five points for assisting a classmate in need,Ó Snape continued, and then he began to proceed to the next table. ŇAnd Mr. PotterÉÓ he said, pausing. ŇDetention for nearly concocting something potentially fatal in my classroom. See me after class.Ó Harry would have ranted and raved about how unfair it was that he received detention when other peopleŐs potions looked like death and heŐd actually managed to succeed, but he was still too dumbfounded at having been awarded points by Snape. HeŐd just won points for bloody Slytherin. Harry had been expecting SnapeŐs Slytherin bias to shine through and to finally have a Potions class where he wouldnŐt lose points, but Snape had actually given him points. ŇYou look like a fish, Potter. Close your mouth,Ó Malfoy muttered. ŇTraitor.Ó The comment came from the other side of the room with a poor attempt at hiding the word with a cough. Harry immediately came out of his stupor and looked over to the Gryffindor side, but he realized he couldnŐt even tell the gender of the voice that had said the word. He did note, however, that Ron looked furious. ŇDo you know who said that?Ó Malfoy whispered as he bottled up his potion sample. Harry shook his head. ŇWell, we should make an effort to find out who it was.Ó Harry shrugged. ŇItŐs not important. TheyŐll get over it,Ó he said, reaching for his own flagon. Malfoy narrowed his eyes. ŇWe will figure it out,Ó he said, apparently ignoring HarryŐs comment. Harry finished filling up his flagon and stoppered it. Snape promptly assigned them an essay on the properties of powdered moonstone, and as everyone began to clean up their tables Harry quickly crossed the room. ŇHey,Ó he said to Ron and Hermione. Hermione smiled, but Ron wouldnŐt even look at him. ŇCan we talk today? Maybe after dinner?Ó Hermione nodded. ŇLetŐs meet in the library,Ó she said. ŇCan we justÉ go to the courtyard?Ó Harry asked. ŇBetween all the fumes in here and then having to be in TrelawneyŐs stuffy classroom after lunch, I have a feeling IŐm going to need some fresh air.Ó ŇOf course, Harry.Ó Ron, without a second glance, finished gathering up his things and left. Harry sighed. ŇI guess IŐll see him in Divination,Ó Harry mused, feeling a little dejected. ŇMaybe heŐll speak to me then.Ó Hermione gave him a small smile, and then a quick hug. ŇItŐll be okay, Harry,Ó she said. ŇYou know Ron. Let him fume, and heŐll come around.Ó ŇRight,Ó Harry said flatly. ŇAre you okay, Harry?Ó ŇIŐm fine.Ó ŇPotter, up front,Ó Snape said. ŇGoodbye, Granger.Ó Hermione shot Harry a look of sympathy and then left. Harry sighed and went to the front of the classroom to face Snape. ŇYou will serve detention with me on Wednesday night,Ó Snape said without looking at Harry, instead choosing to inspect some of the flagons that had been turned in. ŇAnd you will henceforth follow Mr. MalfoyŐs example of copying down all of the instructions prior to beginning to brew a potion.Ó Harry nodded. ŇI canŐt hear you, Potter.Ó ŇYes, sir.Ó ŇGood. Now leave.Ó Harry supposed that despite actually gaining points from Snape, their relationship really wasnŐt going to change all that much. *** After lunch, Harry went to Divination. For the first time that day, he didnŐt have another Slytherin to walk with since none of them took the subject. He arrived and realized that his usual seat at his table with Ron had been taken by Dean Thomas. Harry sighed in disappointment but then was pleased to discover that the only open seat would result in him partnering with Neville. ŇHey,Ó Harry said quietly as he sat down. Neville offered Harry a nervous smile. ŇHi, Harry.Ó Harry realized he had no idea what else to say. Thankfully, Neville did. ŇAre you doing okay?Ó he asked. ŇIn Slytherin, I mean?Ó Harry nodded. ŇYeah,Ó he said truthfully. ŇBetter than I could have expected it to go, at least so far.Ó ŇThe Slytherins arenŐtÉ I meanÉ are theyÉÓ Neville stammered. Harry smiled, knowing what Neville was trying to say. ŇTheyŐve beenÉ surprisingly friendly.Ó He rolled his eyes. ŇHonestly, theyŐre being nicer to me thanÉ others. ThatŐs awful.Ó Harry shrugged. Trelawney, like the others, began with a lecture on O.W.L.s, but unlike the others, she quickly explained that a written test was not an appropriate way to determine oneŐs Ôinner eye.Ő She immediately launched into the dayŐs lesson, which was dream interpretation. She instructed that they were to read the assigned chapter and then attempt to interpret a recent dream of their partnerŐs. Harry sighed, probably a bit too dramatically, after he finished his reading. ŇI had an absolutely awful dream the night before leaving for Hogwarts,Ó he said. ŇI was wandering all through my house looking for my grandmother, but I couldnŐt find her. He told Neville that he'd dreamt of being chased by a stuffed animal anteater that could electrocute people with its snout. Neville was at a complete loss in his interpretation of Harry's fake dream, likely because Neville had been raised by wizards and could barely pronounce "electricity," but also because Harry had completely made it up. By then class was just wrapping up, and Trelawney assigned them all to begin keeping dream journals before the students began to depart. ŇCan I talk to you?Ó he asked Ron quietly. Dean looked at Harry with wide eyes, then glanced at Ron, and quickly departed. Ron and Harry stared at each for a few long moments. ŇWhat is it you wanna say?Ó Ron finally said, breaking the silence. ŇYouŐve barely even looked at me since yesterday,Ó Harry said. ŇI justÉ I need to knowÉÓ He stopped, sighed, and tried again. ŇRon -Ó ŇHarry, justÉ give me time, okay?Ó Ron said, throwing his hands up. ŇI need to think aboutÉ some things.Ó Harry felt an ache open up in his chest, but at the same time, Ron wasnŐt immediately writing him off. ŇOkay,Ó he agreed quietly. ŇI guessÉ IŐll see ya,Ó Ron said, clearly uncomfortable. He gathered up his things and stood up. ŇRon, wait.Ó ŇHarry, I just said -Ó ŇItŐs notÉ itŐs not you whoŐs been low-key calling me a Ôtraitor,Ő is it?Ó Harry said. ŇI didnŐtÉ I didnŐt do this on purpose, you know.Ó Ron sighed. ŇItŐs not me,Ó he said. ŇIŐve gotta go.Ó And then he was gone, leaving Harry alone with Trelawney. ŇI see a very dark future ahead of you, Mr. Potter,Ó Trewlawney said. ŇYou have many troubling choices that you will need to make. You need to be cautious.Ó Harry rolled his eyes and didnŐt even bother with a response before he left. It looked like his relationship with Trelawney would also remain unchanged. *** Transfiguration was also relatively uneventful. Harry was relieved because the work was difficult, and he was grateful for the lack of distractions. ŇIf they tell me to start ramming into people, IŐm not playing for them.Ó *** Harry left the courtyard feeling satisfied. After his rocky conversation with Hermione the previous night, heŐd been more than a little nervous, but their talk had felt familiar and comfortable. As he walked back to the Slytherin common room, his mind kept running back and forth between Umbridge and Quidditch. Hermione had warned him to not act out in UmbridgeŐs class - that she would likely be out for Harry, personally, after all of the tripe that had been published in the Prophet over the summer - but Harry wondered if the Slytherin class would go differently than the GryffindorŐs had. As for Quidditch, Harry realized that Hermione was right - he loved Quidditch, and just because heŐd lost Gryffindor didnŐt mean he had to lose the feeling of flying on his Firebolt, especially when the offer had already been made. He also had to admit that agreeing to play Seeker would likely score him some approval points within Slytherin, and could possibly ease his transition into his new house. At the same time, he knew Ron would be furious, and since Ron still had to Ôthink about some things,Ő Harry worried that playing Seeker for Slytherin would destroy what little chance he had at salvaging their friendship. Harry turned the corner and began to descend the stairs leading to the dungeons, mulling over what he should do. About halfway down, something suddenly wrapped around HarryŐs ankles and he pitched forward down the stairs. He landed on his shoulder and tumbled the rest of the way down. He groaned in pain when he finally came to a stop at the bottom, and he reached up to clutch his shoulder. He craned his neck to peer up the stairs, but he realized his glasses had come off somewhere in the fall. His shoulder throbbed painfully, to the point that he realized he would probably have to go to the hospital wing. Someone suddenly came into view, standing over him.
